title: Mahshi cromb
subtitle: Egyptian stuffed cabbage leaves in tomato sauce
excerpt: Cabbage leaves blanched until pliable, filled with spiced rice and beef, then rolled and packed tightly into a pot with tomato and simmered until the filling is cooked and the leaves have absorbed the sauce. Egyptian stuffed vegetables (mahshi) are made with cabbage, courgette, aubergine, and peppers. The cabbage version is the most forgiving.
sourceNotes: Mahshi (from the Arabic meaning 'stuffed') is the Egyptian term for all stuffed vegetable dishes. Mahshi cromb (cabbage) is made alongside mahshi kousa (courgette), mahshi felfel (pepper), and mahshi warak dawali (grape leaves). The filling of spiced rice and minced meat is consistent across all versions. It is a celebratory dish requiring patience.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rolls can be assembled up to 1 day ahead and refrigerated. Cook when needed.

content.text: The cabbage must be blanched just enough to be pliable without being cooked — the leaves will finish cooking in the pot. Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Lower the whole cabbage in and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until the outer leaves loosen. Remove and peel them off. Return to the boiling water and repeat until you have 16 to 20 usable leaves.

Use raw rice in the filling: it cooks in the steam inside the roll during the simmering stage. If you use par-cooked rice, the rolls will be overcooked by the time the cabbage is tender. Pack the rolls tightly in the pot so they hold each other in shape and do not unravel.

Blanch the {{cabbage-savoy}} as described above to obtain pliable leaves. Cut out the thick central rib from each leaf. Mix the {{short-grain-rice}}, {{beef-mince}}, {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{olive-oil}} together. Place 2 heaped tablespoons of filling along the bottom edge of a cabbage leaf. Fold the sides in and roll firmly into a cylinder. Repeat until all the filling is used. In a wide pot, mix the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{lemon-juice}}, and 200 ml of water. Arrange any unused cabbage leaves on the base of the pot. Pack the rolls tightly in a single layer (or two layers if needed), seam-side down. Pour the tomato mixture over. Place a heavy plate on top to keep the rolls submerged. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 50 to 60 minutes until the rice and meat are cooked through. Serve with yoghurt.

items.cause: Not packed tightly enough or seam not placed down
items.fix: Pack the rolls as tightly as possible in the pot with no gaps. Always place seam-side down. The heavy plate on top prevents them from floating and unravelling

items.symptom: Rice still raw after cooking time
items.cause: Not enough liquid or heat too low
items.fix: The rolls must be submerged. Add more water if needed. Check at 50 minutes and continue in 10-minute increments if the rice still has bite

items.cause: Not blanched long enough or rib not removed
items.fix: The rib must be cut out before rolling — it is too rigid. Blanch the leaves until completely pliable, not just slightly softened

content.text: Mahshi is the Egyptian celebration of stuffed vegetables: cabbage, courgette, aubergine, peppers, and grape leaves all filled with the same spiced rice and meat mixture. It is a dish that takes time and care, made for family meals and gatherings. Egyptian grandmothers are measured partly by the tightness and neatness of their mahshi rolls. The cabbage version is considered the easiest because the leaves are large and forgiving. Serve with plain yoghurt and a salad.
